items.name: Agent
items.docs: Supported AI agents.

items.name: copilot_hook_entries
items.docs: Copilot hook entries shared by global and project registration.

items.name: ensure_claude_hook_entry
items.signature: fn ensure_claude_hook_entry(hooks: &mut Map<String, Value>, event: &str, command: &str) -> bool
items.docs: Returns `true` if a new entry was added, `false` if already registered.

items.name: ensure_codex_hook_entry
items.signature: fn ensure_codex_hook_entry(hooks: &mut Map<String, Value>, event: &str, command: &str) -> bool
items.docs: Returns `true` if a new entry was added, `false` if already registered.

items.name: ensure_gemini_hook_entry
items.signature: fn ensure_gemini_hook_entry(hooks: &mut Map<String, Value>, event: &str, command: &str) -> bool
items.docs: Returns `true` if a new entry was added, `false` if already registered.

items.name: ensure_kiro_hook_entry
items.signature: fn ensure_kiro_hook_entry(hooks: &mut Map<String, Value>, event: &str, entry: &Value) -> bool
items.docs: Returns `true` if a new entry was added, `false` if already registered.

items.name: kiro_hook_entries
items.docs: Kiro hook entries for all supported events.

items.name: merge_kiro_hooks
items.docs:
Merge Kiro hook entries into a JSON config, returning the list of newly added events.

Also ensures the required `name` field is present (Kiro validates it on load).
Returns `(changed, added_events)` — `changed` is true if any field was
inserted (not just hooks), so the caller knows to save the file.

items.name: register_copilot_hooks
items.docs: Register hooks in a project-level Copilot hooks directory (`.github/hooks/`).

items.name: register_copilot_hooks_global
items.docs: Register hooks in the global Copilot config file (`~/.copilot/config.json`).

items.name: register_kiro_hooks
items.docs: Register hooks by creating a Kiro agent file (`.kiro/agents/symposium.json`).

items.name: unregister_copilot_hooks
items.docs: Remove symposium hooks from a Copilot project hooks directory.

items.name: unregister_copilot_hooks_global
items.docs: Remove symposium hooks from the global Copilot config.

items.name: unregister_flat_hooks
items.docs:
Remove symposium hooks from a JSON config where entries are flat objects
with the command in `command_key` (e.g., `"command"` for Kiro, `"bash"` for Copilot).

Contrasts with `unregister_settings_hooks` which handles the nested
`{ "hooks": [{ "command": "..." }] }` structure used by Claude/Gemini/Codex.

items.name: unregister_kiro_hooks
items.docs: Remove the symposium agent file from a Kiro agents directory.

items.name: unregister_settings_hooks
items.docs:
Remove symposium hooks from a Claude/Gemini settings.json file.
Shared by both Claude and Gemini since they use the same structure.

items.name: UpsertResult
items.docs: Result of upserting an MCP server entry.

items.name: register_claude_mcp_servers
items.docs: Claude Code: `mcpServers.<name>` in settings.json

items.name: register_codex_mcp_servers
items.docs: Codex CLI: `[mcp_servers.<name>]` in config.toml

items.name: register_copilot_mcp_servers
items.docs: Copilot: top-level `<name>` in mcp.json

items.name: register_gemini_mcp_servers
items.docs: Gemini CLI: same format as Claude (`mcpServers.<name>`)

items.name: register_goose_mcp_servers
items.docs: Goose: `extensions.<name>` in config.yaml (string manipulation to preserve comments)

items.name: register_json_mcp_servers
items.signature: fn register_json_mcp_servers(config_path: &Path, servers: &[McpServer], container_key: Option<&str>, out: &Output) -> Result<()>
items.docs:
Register MCP servers into a JSON config file under a given container key.

If `container_key` is `Some("mcpServers")`, entries go under `config["mcpServers"][name]`.
If `None`, entries go at the top level `config[name]`.

items.name: register_kiro_mcp_servers
items.docs: Kiro: `mcpServers.<name>` in mcp.json

items.name: register_opencode_mcp_servers
items.docs: OpenCode: `mcp.<name>` in opencode.json

items.name: server_name
items.docs: Extract the name from any McpServer variant.

items.name: server_to_json
items.docs:
Convert an McpServer to the JSON value agents expect in their config.

Stdio: `{"command": "...", "args": [...], "env": [...]}`
Http/Sse: `{"url": "...", "headers": [...]}`

`env` and `headers` are omitted when empty.

items.name: unregister_json_mcp_servers
items.signature: fn unregister_json_mcp_servers(config_path: &Path, names: &[&str], container_key: Option<&str>, out: &Output) -> Result<()>
items.docs: Remove MCP server entries from a JSON config file.

items.name: upsert_json_mcp_entry
items.signature: fn upsert_json_mcp_entry(container: &mut Value, name: &str, expected: &Value) -> UpsertResult
items.docs: Upsert a single MCP server entry into a JSON object container.

items.name: Cli
items.docs: Parsed CLI arguments.

items.name: builtin_audience
items.docs:
Audience section a built-in subcommand belongs to in `--help`.

`None` means the subcommand is hidden (omitted from help entirely).
Plugin-vended subcommands carry their own audience on the manifest;
this only covers the static `Commands` variants above.

items.name: run
items.signature: fn run(sym: &mut Symposium, cmd: Commands, cwd: &Path, out: &Output) -> Result<()>
items.docs:
Run a parsed CLI command.

`cwd` is the working directory for commands that need it (sync, start, crate).
The binary passes `std::env::current_dir()`; tests pass the fixture workspace root.

items.name: AgentEntry
items.docs: An `[[agent]]` entry — just identifies an agent by name.

items.name: AutoUpdate
items.docs: Auto-update behavior for the symposium binary.

items.name: DefaultsConfig
items.docs: Controls which built-in plugin sources are enabled.

items.name: HookScope
items.docs: Where agent hooks are installed.

items.name: PluginSourceConfig
items.docs: A configured plugin source — either a git repository or a local path.

items.name: ResolvedPluginSource
items.docs: A plugin source together with its base directory for resolving relative paths.

items.name: Symposium
items.docs:
Full application context: parsed config + resolved directory paths.

Thread `&Symposium` through all call sites instead of using global state.

items.name: default_home
items.docs: Returns the default symposium home directory (~/.symposium).

items.name: load_config_from
items.docs: Load config from a config directory.

items.name: resolve_cache_dir
items.docs: Resolve cache dir from environment.

items.name: resolve_config_dir_from_env
items.docs: Resolve config dir from environment variables.

items.name: resolve_logs_dir
items.docs:
Resolve logs dir from environment.

Resolution: SYMPOSIUM_HOME/logs → XDG_STATE_HOME/symposium/logs → config_dir/logs.

items.name: DispatchResult
items.docs: Result of dispatching a command.

items.name: dispatch_crate
items.signature: fn dispatch_crate(_sym: &Symposium, name: &str, version: Option<&str>, cwd: &Path) -> DispatchResult

items.name: CrateSkillMetadata
items.docs: Parsed `[package.metadata.symposium]` section.

items.name: SkillSource
items.docs: Where a crate's skills live.

items.name: parse_crate_metadata
items.docs:
Parse the `[package.metadata.symposium]` section from a Cargo.toml file.

Returns:
- `Ok(None)` if there is no `[package.metadata.symposium]` section.
- `Ok(Some(meta))` if the section is present (even if `skills = []`).
- `Err(...)` on malformed entries.

items.name: parse_crate_metadata_str
items.docs: Parse from a TOML string (for testing).

items.name: FetchResult
items.docs: Result of fetching a crate's sources.

items.name: RustCrateFetch
items.docs: Builder for accessing Rust crate source code.

items.name: normalize_crate_name
items.docs:
Normalize a crate name for hyphen/underscore-insensitive comparison.

Cargo treats `foo-bar` and `foo_bar` as the same crate name (published
name on crates.io vs. Rust module identifier), so any name-equality check
against a user-supplied query should go through this normalization.

items.name: WorkspaceCrate
items.docs: A crate in the workspace's dependency graph.

items.name: workspace_crates
items.docs:
Load workspace crates with parsed versions and local path overrides.

Combines dependency resolution and path-override detection into a single
`cargo metadata` call. Path dependencies (those with no registry source)
get their `path` field populated so callers can resolve them locally.

items.name: fetch_via_cargo
items.docs:
Fetch a crate via a temporary dummy cargo package.

`version_req` may be any valid Cargo version requirement (e.g. `"=1.2.3"`,
`"^1.0"`, `"*"`).

items.name: find_crate_in_metadata
items.docs:
Look up the package for `crate_name` in the metadata for `dir` and return
its source path.

items.name: run_cargo_fetch
items.docs: Run `cargo fetch` against the manifest at `dir/Cargo.toml`.

items.name: write_dummy_package
items.docs: Write a minimal package at `dir` that depends on `crate_name = version_req`.

items.name: HUMANS_HEADING
items.docs:
Section headings for the audience-grouped help. Referenced by the
SessionStart discovery hint (`crate::hook`) too, so the rendered help and
the prompt that points agents at it can't drift apart.

items.name: collect_section
items.signature: fn collect_section(cmd: &Command, registry: &PluginRegistry, deps: &[(String, Version)], target: Audience) -> Vec<(String, String)>
items.docs: Collect entries for one audience section: clap's builtins first (sorted), then plugin-vended subs whose predicates apply (sorted).

items.name: help_text
items.signature: fn help_text(parse: Result<&Cli, &Error>, args: &[String], sym: &Symposium, cwd: &Path) -> Option<String>
items.docs:
If this invocation is a help request, return the help text to print.

Returns `None` when the user wants to actually run something.

- no subcommand, or the bare `help` keyword -> top-level
- `<built-in> --help` (incl. nested and required-arg commands) -> clap's own per command help, re-rendered;
- a plugin-vended `<name> --help` -> `None`, so dispatch forwards `--help` to the child.

items.name: subcommand_help
items.docs: Render clap's help for the deepest built-in subcommand named in `args`, or `None` if none is present (top-level invocation, or a plugin name).

items.name: ResolvedHook
items.docs:
A hook prepared for dispatch — installation names looked up to concrete
`Installation` entries, so the dispatch loop never has to scan the plugin's
installations list again.

items.name: discovery_hint
items.docs:
Suggest `cargo agents --help` when the active workspace exposes crate-aware plugin subcommands.
Reuses the help renderer's `applicable_subcommands`, so the hint fires only when there is actually something to discover; `None` otherwise.

items.name: dispatch_builtin
items.docs: Built-in hook logic on canonical symposium types.

items.name: dispatch_plugin_hooks
items.signature: fn dispatch_plugin_hooks(sym: &Symposium, host_agent: HookAgent, event: HookEvent, sym_input: &InputEvent, original_input: &dyn AgentHookInput, prior_output: Value) -> Result<Value, Vec<u8>>
items.docs:
Dispatch plugin hooks with format routing.

Accumulates output as `serde_json::Value` in the host agent's wire format.
When a plugin's format matches the host agent, input/output pass through directly.
When formats differ, conversion goes through symposium canonical types.

Returns `Ok(json)` on success, `Err(stderr)` on exit code 2.

items.name: dispatched_hooks_for_payload
items.signature: fn dispatched_hooks_for_payload(plugins: &[ParsedPlugin], input: &InputEvent, host_agent: HookAgent) -> Vec<ResolvedHook>
items.docs:
Match plugin hooks against the incoming event, selecting at most one hook
per plugin based on format priority:
1. A hook whose format matches the host agent (native fidelity).
2. A symposium-format hook (portable fallback).
3. Otherwise, nothing fires for that plugin.

The resulting `ResolvedHook`s are ready to dispatch without further plugin
lookups.

items.name: execute_hook
items.signature: fn execute_hook(sym: &Symposium, agent: HookAgent, event: HookEvent, input: &str) -> Result<Vec<u8>>
items.docs:
Core hook pipeline: sync → parse → builtin → plugins → serialize.

Takes the raw agent wire-format input, returns agent wire-format output bytes.
Called by both `run()` (CLI) and the test harness.

items.name: handle_post_tool_use
items.docs: Handle PostToolUse: no-op for now.

items.name: handle_session_start
items.docs:
Handle SessionStart: orient the agent toward crate-aware tooling and, when due, nudge the
user to update. The two fragments are computed independently -- the discovery hint is never gated
behind the update-check throttle -- then joined into a single context block.

items.name: handle_user_prompt_submit
items.docs: Handle UserPromptSubmit: no-op for now.

items.name: merge
items.docs:
Recursively merge two JSON objects, with `b` taking precedence over `a`.
Fields with null values in `b` will delete the corresponding field in `a`.
Fields not present in `b` will be left unchanged in `a`.

items.name: run
items.signature: fn run(sym: &Symposium, agent: HookAgent, event: HookEvent) -> ExitCode
items.docs: CLI entry point: read payload from stdin, dispatch, print output.

items.name: run_auto_sync
items.docs:
Run sync if we're in a workspace directory and auto-sync is enabled. Non-fatal.

Uses per-workspace state to skip sync when `Cargo.lock` hasn't changed
since the last successful sync, avoiding expensive `cargo metadata` calls
on every hook invocation.

items.name: update_nudge
items.docs:
Nudge the user to update. Gated by `auto-update = \"warn\"`, the 25h throttle,
and a newer published version on the registry; `None` otherwise.

items.name: write_hook_trace
items.signature: fn write_hook_trace(agent: HookAgent, event: HookEvent, input: &str, output: &[u8])
items.docs:
If `SYMPOSIUM_HOOK_TRACE` is set to a file path, append a JSONL entry.
Used for integration testing to check what hooks occur when we invoke the agent.

items.name: Agent
items.docs: Represents an agent that can handle hook events.

items.name: AgentHookEvent
items.docs:
Represents the "handler" for a specific kind of hook event
(e.g., a `PreToolUse` event coming from `claude`),
capable of parsing the native payloads and outputs
as well as converting from symposium types.

items.name: AgentHookInput
items.docs: Represents the data sent *from* an agent *to* a hook.

items.name: AgentHookOutput
items.docs: Represents the data sent *from* a hook *to* an agent.

items.name: ErasedAgentHookEvent
items.docs:
Erased version of [`AgentHookEvent`]. Represents the "handler" for a
specific kind of hook event (e.g., a `PreToolUse` event coming from `claude`),
capable of parsing the native payloads and outputs as well as
converting from symposium types.

items.name: HookAgent
items.docs: Agents supported by Symposium hooks.

items.name: erase_agent_hook_event
items.docs: Helper to erase a concrete `AgentHookEvent` into a trait object.

items.name: InitOpts
items.docs: Options that can be provided on the command line to skip interactive prompts.

items.name: find_workspace_root
items.docs: Find the workspace root using `cargo metadata`, run from the given directory.

items.name: init
items.signature: fn init(sym: &mut Symposium, out: &Output, opts: &InitOpts) -> Result<()>
items.docs:
Run user-wide initialization.

Prompts for agents (unless provided), writes
`~/.symposium/config.toml`, and registers global hooks.

items.name: interactive
items.docs: Whether we can prompt the user interactively.

items.name: resolve_agents
items.signature: fn resolve_agents(opts: &InitOpts, existing: &[AgentEntry], should_prompt: bool) -> Result<Vec<Agent>>
items.docs:
Resolve which agents to configure. Priority:
1. Explicit `--add-agent` / `--remove-agent` flags (applied to existing set)
2. Interactive multi-select (if `should_prompt`), pre-selecting existing agents
3. Default to first agent (Claude) in non-interactive mode

items.name: install_requirement
items.docs:
Acquire an installation as a requirement: run its kind-specific source
step (if any), then any declared `install_commands`. Does NOT resolve to
a runnable — requirements are only ever "ensure on disk".

items.name: resolve_runnable
items.signature: fn resolve_runnable(sym: &Symposium, installation: &Installation, override_executable: Option<&str>, override_script: Option<&str>, label: &str) -> Result<Runnable>
items.docs:
Acquire an installation's source (if any), run its `install_commands`, and
pick a `Runnable` from (`installation.executable`/`script`) with the
caller's overrides used when the installation leaves them unset.

`label` is the caller's identifier for error messages (e.g. `"hook `foo`"`
or `"subcommand `bar`"`). Used purely for diagnostics; does not affect
resolution.

Validation (in `plugins.rs`) guarantees that across the installation and
the caller-side overrides, at most one of `executable`/`script` is set,
so this function does not re-check that invariant.

items.name: run_install_commands
items.docs:
Run a list of post-install shell commands sequentially. Stops at the first
failure.

items.name: Output
items.docs: Controls whether user-facing status messages are printed.

items.name: display_path
items.docs: Replace the home directory prefix with `~` for display.

items.name: Audience
items.docs:
Whether a subcommand is intended for human or agent use.

Controls grouping in `cargo agents --help`; does not gate dispatch.

items.name: CRATE_DEFAULT_SKILLS_PATH
items.docs: Default subdirectory used when no `[package.metadata.symposium]` is present.

items.name: DirectoryType
items.docs: What type of directory this is (plugin or skill).

items.name: Hook
items.docs:
A validated hook definition.

`command` is the name of an `Installation` in the plugin (possibly a
synthetic one promoted from an inline declaration). `executable` / `script`
/ `args` may further specify the invocation when not pinned by the
installation.

items.name: HookFormat
items.docs:
The wire format a plugin hook expects for input/output.

This is distinct from `HookAgent` because:
- `Symposium` is a wire format but not an agent (no CLI invokes hooks
  in symposium format natively).
- Not all agents have hook wire formats (e.g., Goose uses MCP extensions,
  OpenCode uses JS plugins), so only agents with shell-hook JSON formats
  appear here.

items.name: Installation
items.docs:
A `[[installations]]` entry in the validated `Plugin`.

Inline references on hooks and on other installations are promoted to
synthetic entries here, so this is the single source of truth: every
`Hook.command` and `Hook.requirements` / `Installation.requirements`
names a member of `Plugin.installations`.

Installations may be runnable (have `executable` or `script`), pure setup
(only `install_commands`), pure aggregators (only `requirements`), or any
combination. Whether an installation is *expected* to resolve to a runnable
is decided at the hook layer.

items.name: LoadWarning
items.docs: A non-fatal plugin source load failure.

items.name: MAX_SUBCOMMAND_DESCRIPTION_LEN
items.docs: Maximum length (bytes) of a subcommand's `description` field.

items.name: McpServerEntry
items.docs: An MCP server entry in a plugin manifest.

items.name: ParsedPlugin
items.docs: A parsed plugin with its path and manifest.

items.name: Plugin
items.docs:
A loaded, *validated* plugin manifest.

This is a table of contents — it describes what skills and hooks are
available, but does not load skill content. The skills layer handles
discovery and loading.

items.name: PluginMcpServer
items.docs:
An MCP server entry with optional crate filtering.

When `crates` is present, the server is only registered if the workspace
matches those predicates (ANDed with plugin-level `crates`).

items.name: PluginRegistry
items.docs:
Loaded plugin registry: plugins from TOML manifests and standalone skills
discovered directly in plugin source directories.

items.name: PluginSource
items.docs:
Source declaration for a skill group.

Accepts one of:
- `source.path = "..."` — local path
- `source.git = "..."` — GitHub URL
- `source = "crate"` — skills live in crate source trees (layout controlled
  by `[package.metadata.symposium]` in each crate's Cargo.toml)

`source = "crate"` is the only valid crate form. The former
`source.crate = { ... }` and `source.crate_path = "..."` are parse errors
with a migration hint.

items.name: RESERVED_SUBCOMMAND_NAMES
items.docs:
Names a plugin subcommand cannot use because they collide with built-in
`cargo agents` commands.

items.name: RawInlineInstallation
items.docs:
Inline installation table. Carries the same fields as a
`[[installations]]` entry minus `name`.

items.name: RawInstallationRef
items.docs:
Raw command reference as it appears in TOML: a string (named installation
reference) or an inline installation table.

Inline forms are promoted at validation time into synthetic
`[[installations]]` entries, so the validated `Plugin` only ever stores
installation references as plain names.

items.name: RawNamedInstallation
items.docs: `[[installations]]` entry: a name plus the same fields as a `RawInlineInstallation`.

items.name: RawPluginManifest
items.docs: Raw TOML manifest deserialized from a plugin `.toml` file.

items.name: RawSubcommand
items.docs:
Raw `[subcommand.<name>]` entry. The TOML table-key is the subcommand
name; this struct carries the table body.

items.name: SkillGroup
items.docs:
A `[[skills]]` entry from a plugin manifest.

Each group declares which crates it advises on (`crates`) and
optionally a remote source for the skill files.

items.name: SourceDirContents
items.docs: Raw scan results from a plugin source directory.

items.name: StandaloneSkill
items.docs:
A standalone skill in the registry, paired with the origin it should be
attributed to (derived from the source name + the skill's path within
that source, so two registries can each contribute a same-named
standalone skill without colliding).

items.name: Subcommand
items.docs:
A validated `[subcommand.<name>]` entry.

`command` is the name of an `Installation` in the plugin (possibly a
synthetic one promoted from an inline declaration), matching the same
resolution pattern as `Hook.command`.

items.name: ValidationKind
items.docs: The kind of item that was validated.

items.name: ValidationResult
items.docs: Result of validating a single item in a plugin source directory.

items.name: check_crate_exists
items.docs: Check whether a crate name exists on crates.io.

items.name: collect_crate_names_in_source_dir
items.docs:
Collect all crate names referenced in predicates across a plugin source directory.

Scans TOML plugin manifests (skill group `crates`) and
standalone SKILL.md files, returning deduplicated crate names.
Items that fail to load are silently skipped.

items.name: discover_directory_type
items.docs:
Determine if a directory contains a plugin or skill.
Returns None if it contains neither.
SYMPOSIUM.toml takes precedence over SKILL.md.

items.name: discover_in_directory
items.signature: fn discover_in_directory(dir: &Path, source_name: &str, source_dir: &Path, plugins: &mut Vec<Result<ParsedPlugin>>, skill_files: &mut Vec<PathBuf>) -> Result<()>
items.docs:
Recursively discover plugins and skills with precedence and pruning.

`source_name` and `source_dir` describe the registry source root —
passed through unchanged on recursion and stamped onto each
discovered `ParsedPlugin`.

items.name: ensure_plugin_sources
items.docs:
Fetch/update git-based plugin sources.

Ensure git-based plugin sources are up to date.

`update` controls freshness checking behavior (see `UpdateLevel`).
Only refreshes sources with `auto-update = true` (unless `update` is `Fetch`).
Path-based sources are skipped (no fetching needed).

items.name: fetch_plugin_source
items.signature: fn fetch_plugin_source(sym: &Symposium, git_url: &str, update: UpdateLevel) -> Result<PathBuf>
items.docs: Fetch a plugin source repository, returning the cached directory path.

items.name: find_plugin
items.docs: Find a plugin by name across all sources.

items.name: list_plugins
items.docs: List all providers and their plugins.

items.name: load_all_plugins
items.docs:
Load all plugins from all configured plugin source directories,
discarding load errors with warnings.

Use `load_registry()` instead if you also need standalone skills.

items.name: load_plugin
items.docs:
Load and validate a single plugin from a TOML manifest.

`source_name` and `source_dir` describe the plugin source the
manifest was found through (used for `SkillOrigin::Source`
attribution at sync time). Standalone callers — like the
`plugin validate` CLI — that don't need origin attribution can pass
an empty string and the manifest's parent directory.

items.name: load_registry
items.docs:
Scan all configured plugin source directories and load the registry.

Discovers TOML plugin manifests and standalone skill directories,
then loads both into a `PluginRegistry`.

items.name: resolve_or_promote
items.signature: fn resolve_or_promote(raw: RawInstallationRef, installations: &mut Vec<Installation>, names: &mut BTreeSet<String>, synth_name: &mut dyn FnMut, ctx: &str) -> Result<String>
items.docs:
Resolve a `RawInstallationRef`. If named, validate against the existing
installations and return the name. If inline, promote the inline body to
a new synthetic `Installation` (named via `synth_name`) appended to
`installations`, and return the synthetic name.

items.name: resolve_plugin_source_dirs
items.docs:
Resolve the directories for all configured plugin sources, paired with
each source's display name (used to attribute standalone skills to a
stable origin).

For `path` sources: resolves relative to the source's `base_dir`, or uses absolute paths as-is.
For `git` sources: computes the cache path under `~/.symposium/cache/plugin-sources/`.

Does no network I/O — just computes paths.

items.name: scan_source_dir
items.docs:
Scan a plugin source directory for TOML plugin manifests and standalone skills.

Discovery rules:
1. Plugin = directory with `SYMPOSIUM.toml` file
2. Skill = directory with `SKILL.md` file
3. Plugin takes precedence over skill in the same directory
4. Once a directory is claimed as plugin/skill, don't recurse into it

`source_name` is the registry source the directory was reached
through; it gets stamped onto each `ParsedPlugin` so origin
attribution can use it later. Callers that don't care about origin
attribution (CLI validation, tests) pass `""`.

items.name: standalone_skill_origin
items.docs:
Build the `SkillOrigin` for a standalone skill discovered at
`skill_md` inside the plugin source rooted at `source_dir`.

Identity is `(source_name, skill_path-relative-to-source-root)`,
which matches the `Source` origin assigned to plugin `source.path`
groups. So a standalone skill at `<source>/foo/SKILL.md` and a
plugin in the same source whose `source.path` points at `foo/`
produce the *same* origin — they describe the same on-disk skill.

items.name: sync_plugin_source
items.docs:
Sync plugin sources.

If `provider` is Some, sync only that provider (ignores auto-update).
If `provider` is None, sync all sources with auto-update = true.

items.name: validate_hook
items.signature: fn validate_hook(raw: RawHook, installations: &mut Vec<Installation>, names: &mut BTreeSet<String>) -> Result<Hook>
items.docs:
Validate a raw hook into a `Hook`, promoting any inline `command` /
`requirements` into synthetic entries on `installations`.

items.name: validate_installation
items.docs:
Validate semantic constraints on an installation that serde alone cannot
express:
- `executable` and `script` are mutually exclusive on a single layer.
- cargo + `git` requires an explicit `executable`, since we can't query
  crates.io to infer one.

items.name: validate_manifest
items.docs:
Convert a raw manifest into a validated `Plugin`.

User-declared `[[installations]]` come first in the resulting list, in
declaration order. Inline references on installations and hooks are
promoted into synthetic entries appended to the same list so that every
validated reference is a plain name.

items.name: validate_skill_groups
items.docs:
Validate skill-group source constraints that serde alone cannot express.

When a group uses `source = "crate"`, at least one non-wildcard predicate
must be reachable (plugin-level or group-level) so Symposium can resolve
concrete crates to fetch.

Valid:
  crates = ["serde"]              + source = "crate"  → fetch serde
  crates = ["*"], group ["serde"] + source = "crate"  → fetch serde
  crates = ["*", "serde"]         + source = "crate"  → fetch serde

Invalid:
  crates = ["*"]                  + source = "crate"  → no concrete crate
  crates = ["*"], group ["*"]     + source = "crate"  → no concrete crate

items.name: validate_source_dir
items.docs:
Validate a directory as a plugin source.

Scans for TOML plugin manifests and standalone SKILL.md files,
attempts to load each, and returns validation results for all items found.

items.name: validate_subcommand
items.signature: fn validate_subcommand(name: String, raw: RawSubcommand, installations: &mut Vec<Installation>, names: &mut BTreeSet<String>) -> Result<Subcommand>
items.docs:
Validate a raw subcommand into a `Subcommand`, promoting any inline
`command` into a synthetic installation entry.

items.name: validate_subcommand_name
items.docs: Validate the user-typed name of a `[subcommand.<name>]` table.

items.name: Predicate
items.docs:
A predicate matching a single crate dependency, optionally constrained by version.

The wildcard `*` always matches — even a workspace with zero dependencies.

items.name: PredicateSet
items.docs: A set of predicates where at least one must match (OR semantics).

items.name: parse
items.docs: Parse a single predicate string.

items.name: parse_comma_separated
items.docs:
Parse a comma-separated predicate string into multiple predicates.

Used for SKILL.md frontmatter where `crates: serde, tokio>=1.0` is a single line.

items.name: union_matched_crates
items.signature: fn union_matched_crates(predicate_sets: &[&PredicateSet], workspace: &[(String, Version)]) -> Vec<(String, Version)>
items.docs:
Union matched crates from multiple predicate sets.

Returns the deduplicated set of concrete crates matched by any level.

items.name: check_upgrade
items.docs:
Check whether a newer version is available.  Returns `Some(latest)`
if an upgrade is available, `None` if we're current or ahead.

items.name: latest_version
items.docs:
Query the registry for the latest published version of symposium
by running `cargo search symposium --limit 1`.

items.name: maybe_check_for_update
items.docs:
Full update check for the binary startup path.

Respects config and the 24-hour throttle.  For `warn`, prints a
nudge.  For `on`, installs the update via `cargo install`, then
returns `true` so the caller can re-exec into the new binary.

items.name: maybe_warn_for_update
items.docs:
Warn-only update check for the library path (`cli::run`).

Respects config and the 24-hour throttle.  Prints a nudge when
`auto-update = "warn"` and a newer version exists.  Does nothing for
`off` or `on` — the binary handles `on` (which needs re-exec).

items.name: parse_cargo_search_output
items.docs:
Parse the version from `cargo search` output.

Expected format: `symposium = "0.3.0"    # AI the Rust Way`

items.name: re_exec
items.docs:
Re-execute the current process (replaces the process via exec on Unix,
spawn-and-exit on Windows).

items.name: self_update
items.docs: Run the self-update: check for a newer version and install it.

items.name: RawFrontmatter
items.docs:
Raw frontmatter fields extracted from a SKILL.md file.
`crates` is comma-separated on a single line.

items.name: Skill
items.docs: A parsed skill from a SKILL.md file.

items.name: SkillOrigin
items.docs:
Where a skill came from.

Two skills with equal `SkillOrigin` and equal name install to the same
directory and dedupe; everything else installs independently. This lets
two plugins that legitimately both supply a same-named skill from
different sources coexist, while collapsing the case where two plugins
just happen to point at the same logical bundle.

What matters for identity is *where the skill bytes live*, not which
plugin manifest pointed at them. Two plugins in the same registry
source that both reference the same on-disk skill therefore dedupe.

- `Crate { name, version }` — the skill came from walking the source tree
  of a specific crate version (`source = "crate"`).
  Identity is `(name, version)` only: two plugins targeting the same
  crate version produce the same logical skills regardless of which
  plugin pointed at them.
- `Git { source, commit_sha, skill_path }` — the skill came from a
  `source.git` group. Identity is `(source, commit_sha, skill_path)`:
  `source` is the parsed [`GitSource`](symposium_install::git::GitSource)
  which normalizes equivalent URLs to the same value, the commit SHA
  is the resolved hash that was actually fetched, and `skill_path` is
  the SKILL.md's path within the repo tree. Two URLs that refer to
  the same repository normalize to the same `GitSource` and therefore
  deduplicate. Different SKILL.md files within one repo stay distinct.
- `Source { source_name, skill_path }` — the skill came from a
  plugin's `source.path` group, or from a standalone `SKILL.md` in
  a registry source. `source_name` is the registry source's
  display name (e.g. `"user-plugins"`); `skill_path` is the
  SKILL.md's parent directory relative to the source root, with
  forward slashes. Two plugins in the same source pointing at the
  same on-disk skill bundle therefore produce the same origin.

items.name: SkillWithGroupContext
items.docs:
A skill paired with all predicate sets from its lineage and the origin
it was discovered through.

Each predicate set must match (AND across sets). Within a set,
any predicate matching suffices (OR within a set). An empty
`predicate_sets` vec means "always matches".

items.name: collect_skill_applicable_to
items.signature: fn collect_skill_applicable_to(skill: &Skill, origin: SkillOrigin, plugin_crates: &PredicateSet, group_crates: &PredicateSet, for_crates: &[(String, Version)], results: &mut Vec<SkillWithGroupContext>)
items.docs:
Filter a single skill by crate constraints, pushing it onto `results`
if it matches with its origin attached.

items.name: discover_skills
items.docs:
Discover all skills found in a given directory.

Recursively searches for `SKILL.md` files, then prunes nested candidates
(if `A/SKILL.md` exists, `A/B/SKILL.md` is excluded — skills don't nest).

items.name: fetch_and_resolve_skills
items.signature: fn fetch_and_resolve_skills(crate_name: &str, version_spec: Option<&str>, workspace_crates: &[WorkspaceCrate], group: &SkillGroup, visited: &mut HashSet<String>, skills: &mut Vec<(Skill, SkillOrigin)>, depth: usize)
items.docs: Recursively fetch a crate and resolve its skills via metadata.

items.name: fetch_git_skill_source
items.signature: fn fetch_git_skill_source(sym: &Symposium, git_url: &str) -> Option<(PathBuf, GitSource, String)>
items.docs:
Fetch a `source.git` group's tarball and look up the resolved commit
SHA from the cache meta. Returns `(cache_dir, parsed_source, commit_sha)`
or `None` (with a warning) on failure.

items.name: find_skill_files_recursive
items.docs: Recursively walk a directory collecting paths to `SKILL.md` files.

items.name: load_crate_skills
items.signature: fn load_crate_skills(plugin_crates: &PredicateSet, group_crates: &PredicateSet, group: &SkillGroup, workspace_crates: &[WorkspaceCrate], for_crates: &[(String, Version)]) -> Vec<(Skill, SkillOrigin)>
items.docs:
Resolve crate source predicates, fetch each matched crate, read its
`[package.metadata.symposium]`, and discover skills. Follows redirects
recursively with cycle detection.

items.name: load_git_skills
items.signature: fn load_git_skills(sym: &Symposium, group: &SkillGroup, url: &str) -> Vec<(Skill, SkillOrigin)>
items.docs:
Fetch a `source.git` group's tree and build a per-skill `Git` origin
keyed on `(owner/repo, commit_sha, skill_path-within-repo)` so two
plugins that loaded the same SKILL.md at the same commit collapse —
even if their `source.git` URLs differed.

items.name: load_path_skills
items.signature: fn load_path_skills(dir: &Path, group: &SkillGroup, parsed: &ParsedPlugin) -> Vec<(Skill, SkillOrigin)>
items.docs:
Discover skills under a local `source.path` directory and stamp each
with a `Source` origin keyed on `(source_name, skill-path-relative-to-source-root)`.
Two plugins in the same source pointing at the same on-disk skill
therefore produce the same origin.

items.name: load_skill
items.docs:
Load a single skill from a SKILL.md file.

A skill should have `crates` at either the skill level or
the group level (or both). If neither provides it, a warning is logged
but loading succeeds (the skill simply won't match any crate query).

items.name: load_skills_for_group
items.signature: fn load_skills_for_group(sym: &Symposium, parsed: &ParsedPlugin, group: &SkillGroup, workspace_crates: &[WorkspaceCrate], for_crates: &[(String, Version)]) -> (PredicateSet, Vec<(Skill, SkillOrigin)>)
items.docs:
Discover and load skills for a group, applying pre-fetch filtering.

Checks group-level `crates` predicates against `for_crates` before
fetching git sources, to avoid unnecessary downloads. Each returned
skill is paired with the `SkillOrigin` it was discovered through:

- `CratePath` → `SkillOrigin::Crate { name, version }`, one per
  matched crate (canonical name/version from the fetch result).
- `Git` → `SkillOrigin::Git { source, commit_sha, skill_path }`, one
  per discovered SKILL.md (path varies per skill within the group).
- `Path` → `SkillOrigin::Source { source_name, skill_path }`, with
  `skill_path` computed per discovered SKILL.md relative to the
  plugin source root. Two plugins in the same source pointing at
  the same on-disk skill therefore produce the same origin.

items.name: load_standalone_skill
items.docs:
Load a standalone skill from a SKILL.md file (no plugin group context).

Standalone skills must be self-contained: all metadata (crates)
comes from the SKILL.md frontmatter.
Returns an error if `crates` is missing (standalone skills have
no group to inherit from).

items.name: parse_frontmatter
items.docs: Parse SKILL.md content: extract `---`-fenced frontmatter and body.

items.name: prune_nested_skills
items.docs:
Remove nested skill candidates: if `A/SKILL.md` and `A/B/SKILL.md` both
exist, keep only the shallower `A/SKILL.md`.

items.name: skill_path_relative_to
items.docs:
SKILL.md's parent directory relative to a given root, normalized to
forward slashes. Canonicalizes both ends first so that two routes
to the same on-disk skill (e.g. `plugin-a/../shared/the-skill` vs.
the standalone walk's direct `shared/the-skill`) collapse to the
same string.

Falls back to the unnormalized path on canonicalization failure
(better than panicking) — that just degrades dedup to per-route
without losing correctness.

items.name: skill_path_within_repo
items.docs:
Compute a SKILL.md's path relative to the *repository root*, using
the cache directory (which holds the subtree the user pointed at via
`tree/<ref>/<subpath>`) and the source's intra-repo subpath.

Returned with forward-slash separators so the value is stable across
platforms — it's part of the `SkillOrigin::Git` identity.

items.name: skills_applicable_to
items.docs:
Resolve all applicable skills from the registry.

Resolve all skills applicable to the given crates.

`for_crates` is the set of crate name/version pairs to match against.
For `crate --list`, this is the full workspace deps.
For `crate <name>`, this is a single-element slice with the resolved crate.

items.name: CURRENT_VERSION
items.docs: Runtime version of this binary, baked in at compile time.

items.name: ensure_current
items.docs:
Ensure `state.toml` exists and reflects the running binary version.

Returns the previously recorded version (if any) so callers can
decide whether a migration is needed.

items.name: load
items.docs: Load `state.toml` from `config_dir`, returning `None` if absent or unparseable.

items.name: record_update_check
items.docs: Record that an update check just happened.

items.name: should_check_for_update
items.docs: Whether enough time has elapsed since the last update check.

items.name: stamp
items.docs:
Write `state.toml` into `config_dir` with the current binary version,
preserving other fields.

items.name: ExternalOutput
items.docs: Result of running an external subcommand.

items.name: applicable_subcommands
items.signature: fn applicable_subcommands(registry: &PluginRegistry, deps: &[(String, Version)]) -> impl Iterator
items.docs:
Iterate every plugin subcommand whose plugin-level and subcommand-level crate predicate
apply to `deps`. Shared between dispatch (name lookup) and help rendering (audience grouping).

items.name: dispatch_external
items.signature: fn dispatch_external(sym: &Symposium, cwd: &Path, argv: Vec<OsString>) -> Result<ExternalOutput>

items.name: find_subcommand
items.docs:
Look up a subcommand by name across all plugins, filtered by workspace crates at a plugin
 and subcommand levels.

- `Ok(None)` - no plugin claims the name, or every claim was filtered out.
- `Ok(Some(..))` - exactly one plugin claims the name and applies.
- `Err(..)` - two or more plugins claim the name and all apply.

items.name: spawn
items.signature: fn spawn(runnable: Runnable, install_args: &[String], forwarded: &[OsString]) -> Result<ExternalOutput>

items.name: MARKER_FILE
items.docs:
Marker file written into every skill directory symposium installs.

Cleanup walks each agent's skills parent dir and removes any subdir
containing this marker that isn't in the freshly-installed set, leaving
user-managed skill directories (which lack the marker) untouched.

items.name: copy_dir_recursive
items.docs:
Recursively copy the contents of `src` into `dst`. Creates `dst` if
missing. Regular files are copied with `fs::copy`; subdirectories are
walked. Symlinks and other special files are ignored.

items.name: create_managed_dir_all
items.docs:
Create `path` (and any missing ancestors up to `boundary`), writing a
`.gitignore` file containing `*` into each directory we newly create.

`boundary` is the workspace root — we never walk above it, and we do not
write a `.gitignore` there (it already exists and isn't ours to manage).
If `path` or an ancestor already exists, no `.gitignore` is added — we
only annotate directories we actually create.

items.name: discover_user_authored_skills
items.docs:
Discover user-authored skills in `<project_root>/.agents/skills/`.

A skill is user-authored iff its directory contains `SKILL.md` and does
*not* contain the `.symposium` marker. Symposium never writes markers
into source skills, so this unambiguously separates user content from
copies symposium put there itself.

items.name: has_symposium_marker
items.docs:
Does `dir` contain the `.symposium` marker, i.e. is it a symposium-managed
skill directory? Returns `false` for user-authored skills and for any
directory symposium did not create.

items.name: mark_generated_skill_directory
items.docs:
Mark a directory as symposium-generated: drop the `.symposium` marker
and a `.gitignore` containing `*` so the directory is recognized on
future syncs and kept out of version control.

Idempotent — overwrites any pre-existing marker or `.gitignore` in
`dir`. Callers use this both for freshly-installed plugin skills and
for skills propagated by the agents-syncing feature.

items.name: propagate_user_skill
items.signature: fn propagate_user_skill(source_dir: &Path, dest_dir: &Path, project_root: &Path, out: &Output) -> Result<bool>
items.docs:
Propagate a user-authored skill from `.agents/skills/<name>/` to
`dest_dir`, returning `Ok(true)` if propagation happened (so the caller
should record `dest_dir` as installed).

Leaves `dest_dir` alone if it exists and lacks the `.symposium` marker —
the user put something there by hand and we must not clobber it.

items.name: register_hooks
items.docs:
Register global hooks for all configured agents.
Register hooks for all configured agents. Uses `home_dir` (global scope).
Called from `init` after writing the user config.

items.name: skills_parent_dir
items.docs:
Skills parent directory for an agent (e.g. `.claude/skills/` or
`.agents/skills/`), derived from `Agent::project_skill_dir`.

items.name: sync
items.docs:
Run the full sync: discover applicable skills, install into agent dirs,
clean up stale installations.

items.name: find_workspace_root
items.docs:
Find the workspace root via `cargo locate-project --workspace`.
Fast (~10-50ms) and follows cargo's actual workspace discovery logic.

items.name: mtime_matches
items.docs:
Check whether the cached mtime matches the current file state.
A missing file matches a `None` cached mtime (both absent = fresh).
A missing file with a `Some` cached mtime means the file was deleted = stale.

items.name: Decision
items.docs:
Output for a `PreToolUse` event.
Decision for a `PreToolUse` hook.

items.name: HookEvent
items.docs: Hook event types supported by Symposium.

items.name: HookHandler
items.docs:
Trait for implementing a symposium hook handler.

Override the methods for the events you care about. The defaults return
the default (empty) output for each event type.

items.name: Input
items.docs: Input event received on stdin.

items.name: Output
items.docs: Output event written to stdout.

items.name: PostToolUseInput
items.docs: Input for a `PostToolUse` event.

items.name: PostToolUseOutput
items.docs: Output for a `PostToolUse` event.

items.name: PreToolUseInput
items.docs: Input for a `PreToolUse` event.

items.name: SessionStartInput
items.docs: Input for a `SessionStart` event.

items.name: SessionStartOutput
items.docs: Output for a `SessionStart` event.

items.name: UserPromptSubmitInput
items.docs: Input for a `UserPromptSubmit` event.

items.name: UserPromptSubmitOutput
items.docs: Output for a `UserPromptSubmit` event.

items.name: default_handle_event
items.signature: fn default_handle_event(handler: &impl HookHandler + Sized, input: &Input) -> Result<Output>
items.docs:
Default dispatch logic: matches on the event variant and calls the
corresponding method on the handler, wrapping the result in the appropriate
`Output` variant.

items.name: run
items.docs:
Run a hook handler. Reads the input event from stdin, dispatches to the
handler, and writes the output to stdout with the correct exit code.

- Empty output (all fields `None`) → exit 0, no stdout.
- Non-empty output → exit 0, JSON on stdout.
- `Err(...)` → exit 1, error message on stderr.

items.name: AcquiredKind
items.docs: How an [`AcquiredSource`] was obtained.

items.name: AcquiredSource
items.docs:
Acquired source — where files ended up on disk, plus the kind so
`executable` / `script` can be resolved correctly relative to it.

items.name: CargoSource
items.docs: A binary obtained by `cargo install` (with optional binstall fast-path).

items.name: CratesIoCrateResponse
items.docs: Response from crates.io crate endpoint (for getting latest version)

items.name: CratesIoVersionResponse
items.docs: Response from crates.io version endpoint

items.name: GithubSource
items.docs:
A directory of files acquired from a GitHub repository (or subtree).
The file to run inside the cloned tree is picked by `executable` /
`script` on the installation or hook.

items.name: InstallContext
items.docs:
Minimal context needed for acquisition.

Replaces the full `Symposium` config struct — hook handlers construct this
directly from environment or hardcoded paths.

items.name: Runnable
items.docs: What an installation resolves to once acquired.

items.name: Source
items.docs:
How to acquire bits onto disk.

`Source` describes acquisition only: cargo install, github clone. The
"what to run" lives separately on the installation as `executable` or
`script` (which resolve relative to the acquired source). An installation
can omit `Source` entirely — in that case `executable` / `script` are
taken as paths on disk and `install_commands` does any setup.

items.name: UpdateLevel
items.docs: Controls how aggressively cached sources are updated.

items.name: acquire_cargo
items.signature: fn acquire_cargo(ctx: &InstallContext, cargo: &CargoSource, executable_hint: Option<&str>) -> Result<(PathBuf, Option<String>)>
items.docs:
Acquire a cargo installation: install if missing, return the cache dir
plus the resolved binary name (when discoverable from crates.io).

items.name: acquire_github
items.docs:
Acquire a github source: returns the cache directory containing the repo
(or its requested subtree).

items.name: acquire_source
items.signature: fn acquire_source(ctx: &InstallContext, source: &Source, executable_hint: Option<&str>) -> Result<AcquiredSource>
items.docs:
Acquire a source, downloading / installing as needed. The returned
`AcquiredSource` is used to resolve `executable` / `script` paths.

`executable_hint` is only used for cargo (to pick which binary to install
for multi-binary crates, or as the binary name when using a git source).

items.name: git_cache_version
items.docs:
Cache key for a git-sourced cargo install. The user-supplied `version` (or
the literal `"git"` if absent) folds in with the URL so re-installs are
triggered when either changes.

items.name: install_cargo_crate
items.signature: fn install_cargo_crate(ctx: &InstallContext, crate_name: &str, version: &str, binary_name: Option<String>, cache_dir: PathBuf, git: Option<String>) -> Result<()>
items.docs: Install a crate using cargo binstall (fast) or cargo install (fallback).

items.name: install_cargo_crate_sync
items.signature: fn install_cargo_crate_sync(ctx: &InstallContext, crate_name: &str, version: &str, binary_name: Option<String>, cache_dir: &Path, git: Option<String>) -> Result<()>

items.name: make_executable
items.docs: Ensure a path is executable on Unix. No-op on other platforms.

items.name: query_crate_binaries
items.signature: fn query_crate_binaries(crate_name: &str, version: Option<&str>) -> Result<(String, Vec<String>)>
items.docs: Query crates.io for binary names of a crate

items.name: DEBOUNCE_DURATION
items.docs: Minimum interval between freshness checks for cached sources.

items.name: GitCacheManager
items.docs: Manages downloading and caching of git-sourced plugin artifacts.

items.name: GitHubClient
items.docs: Client for GitHub REST API operations.

items.name: GitSource
items.docs:
A parsed git source URL.

Different URL forms that refer to the same logical repository (e.g.,
`https://github.com/foo/bar` and `git@github.com:foo/bar.git`) parse
to the same variant with the same fields, enabling deduplication.

items.name: PluginCacheMeta
items.docs: Metadata stored alongside cached plugin artifacts.

items.name: extract_tarball
items.docs: Extract a gzip tarball, flattening the single top-level directory.

items.name: flatten_single_dir
items.docs:
If a directory contains exactly one subdirectory and nothing else,
move that subdirectory's contents up one level.

items.name: parse_git_url
items.docs:
Parse a git URL into a structured [`GitSource`].

Currently recognizes GitHub URLs:
- `https://github.com/owner/repo`
- `https://github.com/owner/repo/tree/branch/path/to/dir`

items.name: parse_github_https
items.docs: Parse an `https://github.com/...` URL.

items.name: AgentSession
items.docs: A live agent session for the duration of a test.

items.name: FixtureScanResult
items.docs: Directories discovered while copying fixture files.

items.name: HookStep
items.docs:
A single step in a hook scenario. Used by simulation mode to drive
`execute_hook`; ignored in agent mode (the real agent produces its own hooks).

items.name: HookTrace
items.docs: One hook invocation's input and output, in canonical form.

items.name: SubmitResult
items.docs: Collected results from a `submit` call.

items.name: TestAgent
items.docs: Which agent backend to use for integration tests.

items.name: TestContext
items.docs: Test context wrapping an isolated `Symposium` instance.

items.name: TestMode
items.docs: Which modes a test should run in.

items.name: configured_test_agents
items.docs:
Read the list of test agents from `test-agents.toml` at the repo root,
filtered by `SYMPOSIUM_TEST_AGENT` env var if set.

items.name: copy_dir_recursive
items.signature: fn copy_dir_recursive(src: &Path, dst: &Path, scan: &mut FixtureScanResult, vars: &[(&str, &str)])
items.docs: Recursively copy a directory tree, expanding variables in text files.

items.name: expand_vars
items.docs: Expand variables in file content.

items.name: infer_agent_name
items.docs: Infer the symposium agent name from a registry name.

items.name: is_agent_mode
items.docs: Returns `true` when running in agent mode (i.e. `SYMPOSIUM_TEST_AGENT` is set).

items.name: is_text_file
items.docs: File extensions that get variable expansion.

items.name: parse_agent_name
items.docs: Parse an agent name string into a TestAgent.

items.name: run_with_acp_session
items.signature: fn run_with_acp_session(agent: impl ConnectTo, ctx: TestContext, f: &impl AsyncFn) -> Result<()>
items.docs: Establish an ACP connection, create a session, and run the callback inside it.

items.name: setup_fixture
items.docs:
Create a test context by overlaying fixture fragments into a tempdir.

Text files (`.toml`, `.md`, `.json`, `.txt`, `.ts`, `.js`) have variables expanded:
- `$TEST_DIR` — the tempdir root
- `$BINARY` — path to the `cargo-agents` binary (from `CARGO_BIN_EXE_cargo-agents`)

items.name: test_agent
items.docs: Reads `SYMPOSIUM_TEST_AGENT` and returns the agent backend, if set.

items.name: with_fixture
items.signature: fn with_fixture(mode: TestMode, fixtures: &[&str], f: impl AsyncFn) -> Result<()>
items.docs:
Set up a test fixture and run the given closure.

- `SimulationOnly`: runs once in simulation mode.
- `AgentOnly`: runs once per configured test agent.
- `Any`: runs once in simulation + once per configured test agent.
